q: Plants make their own organic molecules, using the process of photosynthesis.
Carbon dioxide is the source of carbon and light is the source of energy.
The main photosynthetic organs are the roots, which have evolved a large surface area to volume ratio.
A large surface area to volume ratio means more area for collection of light and carbon dioxide.
Most plants have compact bodies like animals.
Plants have extensive branching bodies with leaves above the ground.
Plants have extensive root systems below the ground to obtain the water and mineral salts they needed for nutrition.
Water and mineral ions are absorbed by roots and transported through the phloem to other parts of the plant.
Sugars are produced in leaves, but glucose is needed by all parts of the plant for respiration.
Glucose can be moved through the phloem as part of the sucrose molecule.
Sugars usually change into the form of starch for storage in a potato tuber.
The branching shape of plants helps to keep the surface area to volume ratio large.
Energy needs of plants are generally large compared with those of animals.
Plants can rely on diffusion to supply their cells with oxygen and to remove carbon dioxide.
Diffusion is sufficient to supply the mesophyll cells with carbon dioxide for photosynthesis, and to remove oxygen.
Xylem transports water and inorganic ions from the roots to all parts of the plant.
Phloem transports substances made in the plant, such as sucrose and amino acids, from the leaves to all other parts of the plant.
Xylem tissue contains dead, empty hollow cells with no end walls.
Xylem vessels are arranged in long lines to form a hollow tube.
Water moves through xylem tubes by mass flow from the roots to all other parts of the plant.
Phloem tissue contains cells called sieve tube which are dead cells.
Sieve tubes contain cytoplasm and a few organelles but no nucleus.
Sieve tube walls are made of cellulose.
A companion cell is associated with each sieve tube.
Within a plant, mineral ions and organic compounds (e.g., sucrose) are transported by being dissolved in water.
The dissolved mineral ions are transported in the phloem tissue.
The dissolved organic compounds are transported in the phloem tissue.
The plant roots are responsible for the uptake of water and mineral ions and can have root hairs to increase the surface area.
The uptake of water is an active process and occurs by osmosis.
Osmosis is the diffusion of water from a higher water potential to a lower water potential.
The uptake of minerals can be passive transport such as diffusion and osmosis, and/or active transport.
In active absorption, the water first enters the cell sap and passes from one cell to another.
The type of movement where protoplasm is involved is called apoplast.
In passive absorption, water moves through the apoplast of the root.
The apoplast path includes the cell wall and intercellular spaces.
Apoplastic and symplastic pathways initiate the passage of water along with ions from root hair via root cortex to xylem.
Most water travels via the apoplastic pathway when transpiration rates are high.
The apoplastic pathway is the series of spaces running through the cellulose cell walls, dead cells, and the hollow tubes of the xylem.
Water moves by osmosis in the apoplastic pathway because it passes through a partially permeable membrane.
The movement of water through the apoplastic pathway occurs more rapidly than the symplastic pathway.
The Casparian strip is a thick, waterproof, waxy band of suberin within the cell wall of the endodermis.
The Casparian strip forms an impassable barrier for the water.
A smaller amount of water travels via the symplastic pathway, which is the cytoplasm and plasmodesmata or vacuole of the cells.
In the symplastic pathway, water moves by osmosis into the cell across the partially permeable cell surface membrane.
The movement of water in the symplastic pathway is faster than the apoplastic pathway.
The cells in the mesophyll layers are tightly packed and have many spaces around them filled with air.
The walls of the mesophyll cells are wet and some of this water evaporates into the air space.
The air inside the leaf is usually saturated with water vapour.
Water vapour will diffuse out of the leaf down a water potential gradient through stomata.
If the water potential gradient between the air spaces in the leaf and the air outside becomes steeper, the rate of transpiration will increase.
In condition of low humidity, the gradient is steep, so transpiration takes place more quickly than in high humidity.
Transpiration rate may be increased by an increasing in wind speed or rising in temperature.
In most plants, stomata open during the night and close at day.
Most transpiration takes place through the stomata, so the rate of transpiration is almost zero at night.
Closing stomata at night, when photosynthesis is impossible, reduces unnecessary water loss.
In especially dry conditions, a plant may partially or completely close its stomata to prevent its leaves drying out.
As water evaporates from the cell walls inside the leaf, it absorbs heat energy from these cells, thus reducing their temperature.
If the rate at which water vapour is lost by transpiration exceeds the rate at which a plant can take up water from the soil, the cells become more turgid.
When cells become less turgid, the plant wilts as the soft parts such as leaves loose the support provided turgid cells.
Ascent of sap mostly occurs due to transpiration pull exerted by transpiration of water.
Transpiration removes the excess of water absorbed by plants.
Transpiration by evaporating water brings down their temperature by $10^\circ-15^\circ\text{C}$.
The development of mechanical tissue is favoured by the increase in transpiration.
Mineral salts are mostly distributed by rising column of sap.
The sap absorbed from the soil contains high concentration of mineral salts.
The loss of water through transpiration increases the concentration of mineral salts in the plant.
Transpiration helps in better development of root system which is required for support and absorption of mineral salts.
The ash and sugar content of the fruit increases with the increase in transpiration.
Excessive transpiration induces hardening and resistance to moderate drought.
Transpiration helps the shape and structure of plant parts by keeping cells turgid.
Transpiration supplies water for photosynthesis.
Loss of water from the twig produces a vacuum in the thistle funnel and water will be drawn up.
Translocation is the movement of dissolved substances through a plant.
Water and dissolved salts from the soil travel upwards through the vessels of the xylem.
Food synthesized in the leaves passes downwards or upwards in the sieve tubes of the phloem.
When leaves photosynthesize, they produce carbohydrates in the form of sucrose.
Sucrose may travel upwards to actively growing regions or maturing fruits and seeds or downwards to the roots.
Both upward and downward movement may take place at the same time in the phloem.
The phloem tissue is a simple tissue consisting of only sieve tubes.
The components of phloem are sieve tube, companion cell, phloem parenchyma and phloem fibre.
Phloem fibers are the only living cells in the phloem tissue.
Sieve tubes or sieve cells have no nucleus and companion cells possess do work together.
The cell septate walls in phloem are known as sieve plates.
Sieve cells are kept alive by the nucleated companion cells that always situated adjacent to sieve cells.
Phloem tissue is strengthened by fibre cells besides the sieve and companion cells.
Large multicellular animals have evolved transport system as organisms get larger and have higher metabolic rates.
In many large animals, circulatory system is in the form of mass transport system.
Mass transport system is an arrangement of structures by which substances are transported in the flow of a fluid.
Single-celled organisms have a low surface area to volume ratio.
In large multicellular organisms, the surface area to volume ratio is low.
Many chemical reactions take place inside every microscopic cell in multicellular animals.
Respiration supplies energy for the other reactions of life, but it also produces the toxic waste product carbon dioxide.
Mass transport systems have exchange surfaces to get materials into and out of the transport system.
A system of vessels that carry substances are usually tubes, sometimes following a very specific route.
Mass transport systems must have a way of making sure that substances are moved in the right direction.
Moving materials fast enough may involve mechanical methods such as the pumping of the heart.
A suitable transport medium (e.g., fluid) is a feature of most mass transport systems.
There are two main types of circulatory system; open circulatory system and closed circulatory system.
In an open circulatory system, blood pumped by the tubular heart leaves the open-ended vessels to a series of blood spaces surrounding the tissues.
When the heart relaxes in an open system, blood flows back into the vessels through pores called ostia.
The open circulatory system is found in birds and mammals.
In a closed circulatory system, blood is pumped by the heart into the blood vessels which carry the blood to the tissues of the entire body.
The main advantages of a closed system are that pressure can be increased to make blood flow more quickly.
There are two types of closed circulatory system; single circulatory system and double circulatory system.
Single circulatory system is found in fish.
In fish, the heart pumps oxygenated blood to the gills.
In a single circulatory system, blood passes once through the heart.
Double circulatory system involves two separate circulations.
The systemic circulation carries oxygenated blood from the heart to the cells of the body.
The pulmonary circulation carries oxygenated blood from the heart to the lungs.
In a double circulatory system, blood passes twice through the heart.
Birds and mammals need much more oxygen than fish.
Double circulatory system helps birds and mammals maintain a constant body temperature.
The fully oxygenated blood can be delivered quickly to the body tissues at high pressure in a double circulatory system.
The cardiovascular system is made up of the heart, a series of blood vessels and the blood.
The passage of blood through the vessels is called the circulation.
The human heart is a muscular pump with four chambers.
The upper two chambers are called the right atrium and the left atrium.
The walls of the atria are thicker than those of the ventricles.
The right atrium receives deoxygenated blood from the superior vena cava and from the inferior vena cava.
The left atrium receives oxygenated blood from the lungs via pulmonary veins.
Deoxygenated blood in the right ventricle enters the lungs through pulmonary artery.
The oxygenated blood in the left ventricle enters into aorta.
The blood in one side of the heart does not mix with the blood from the other side due to a muscular septum.
Cardiac muscle can carry on contracting regularly without resting or getting fatigued.
Cardiac muscle has a good blood supply by the coronary arteries bringing oxygenated blood.
Myoglobin is a respiratory pigment which has a stronger affinity for oxygen than haemoglobin.
The muscular wall of the left ventricle is much thinner than that of the right.
The right ventricle pumps blood to the lungs, which are relatively close to the heart.
There are four sets of valves in the heart; two sets of atrioventricular valves and two sets of semilunar valves.
The right atrioventricular valve is called tricuspid valves which is made up of three flaps.
The left atrioventricular valve is called bicuspid valve (Mitral valve) and is made up of two flaps.
The function of the bicuspid valve is prevention of backflow of blood from the left ventricle into the left atrium.
Flaps of both atrioventricular valves are supported by the cordae tendineae (tendinous cords).
Semilunar valves are pocket-like valves with half-moon shaped like those in veins.
Arteries carry blood away from the heart towards the cells of the body.
Almost all arteries carry oxygenated blood except the pulmonary artery and the umbilical artery.
The aorta branches off into arteries in every direction.
The very smallest branches of the arterial system are the arterioles.
Artery walls contain a lot of elastic fibers so they can stretch to accommodate pressure surges.
The elastic recoil of artery walls helps to increase pressure.
Veins carry blood back towards the heart and most carry deoxygenated blood.
The pulmonary vein carries oxygen-rich blood from the lungs back to the heart.
The blood pressure in the veins is relatively low.
Veins can hold a large volume of blood, more than half of the body's blood volume.
The walls of the veins are thicker than those of the arteries.
Capillaries have walls that are very thin and contain no elastic fibers, smooth muscle or collagen.
Capillary walls consist of just one very thin cell.
Blood entering the capillary network from the arteries is oxygenated.
Plasma is the liquid part of the blood and over 50% of the blood volume is plasma.
Plasma carries heat around the system from internal organs to the skin.
There are approximately 5 million erythrocytes per $\text{mm}^3$ of blood.
Mature erythrocytes do not contain a nucleus and have a limited life of about 120 days.
Each red blood cell contains around 250-300 million molecules of haemoglobin.
Leucocytes are much smaller than erythrocytes.
Leucocytes can change their shape and some are made in the bone marrow and some in the thymus gland.
Platelets are tiny fragments of large cells called megakaryocytes.
Serotonin causes the smooth muscle of the blood vessel to contract, narrowing the blood vessel.
Thromboplastin is an enzyme that starts a sequence of chemical changes that clot the blood.
Thrombin acts on fibrinogen, converting it to an insoluble substance called fibrin.
The contractions of the heart are called systole and relaxation is called diastole.
Ventricular systole happens about 0.13 seconds after atrial systole.
One cycle of systole and diastole makes up a single heartbeat which lasts about 0.8 seconds in humans.
Cardiac muscle cells are myogenic, meaning they contract without any external stimulus.
The sinoatrial node (SAN) acts as the heart's own natural pacemaker.
The atrioventricular node (AVN) is excited as a result of the SAN but it produces a slight delay.
The bundle of His carries the wave of excitation to the Purkyne tissue.
Electrical changes of the heart can be measured in an electrocardiogram (ECG).
Between the capillaries and the cells is a watery liquid called tissue fluid.
Most of the water from tissue fluid reenters capillaries by osmosis.
The lymphatic system consists of vessels similar to blood capillaries, called lymphatic capillaries.
The lacteals are lymphatic vessels that carry fats from villi of the small intestine.
Lymph is filtered to remove dead cells and bacteria in lymph nodes.
The lymphatic system opens into the subclavian veins to return lymph to the blood.
